title: Working with the part that believes rest is dangerous
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The moment I realized rest wasn't safe 

 I was sitting on my couch. Nothing was happening. No deadlines. No emergencies. No one needed anything from me....
htmlContent:
The moment I realized rest wasn't safe

I was sitting on my couch. Nothing was happening. No deadlines. No emergencies. No one needed anything from me. And I felt like I was going to crawl out of my skin.

My heart was racing. My jaw was clenched. Every muscle in my body was coiled, ready to spring into action for a threat that didn't exist. I told myself to relax. I took deep breaths. I tried to meditate. And the harder I tried, the worse it got.

Here's the thing. I wasn't being dramatic. I wasn't broken. There was a part of me that genuinely believed rest was dangerous. And that part wasn't wrong. Not from its perspective.

I've come to learn that this isn't just me. It's a common experience for people who grew up in environments where vigilance was necessary for survival. Where letting your guard down meant getting hurt. Where the moment you stopped performing, stopped achieving, stopped taking care of everyone else, something bad happened.

That part of you that won't let you rest? It's not lazy. It's not defective. It's a protector. And it's been doing its job for a very long time.

What we know about the part that drives you

Janina Fisher, who writes about structural dissociation and the internal family systems model, talks about how trauma survivors often develop parts that are relentlessly driven. These parts believe that if you stop, you'll collapse. Or that someone will hurt you. Or that you'll feel the weight of everything you've been avoiding.

Fisher calls these "driven parts" or "protector parts." They keep you moving because moving feels safer than stopping. Movement creates the illusion of control. Rest, on the other hand, opens the door to everything you've been running from.

And look, I get it. I've been there. I've worked 60-hour weeks while my body screamed for sleep. I've said yes to every request until I had nothing left. I've felt guilty for taking a Saturday afternoon to do nothing. The part that drives me has saved my life more than once. It got me through situations where stopping would have meant drowning.

But here's what I've learned: that part doesn't know it's not 1998 anymore. Or 1987. Or whatever year your body decided that rest was a luxury you couldn't afford.

Think about it. When you were a kid, was there ever a consequence for relaxing? Did someone get angry when you weren't productive? Did you learn that your worth was tied to what you accomplished? Did you have to be the responsible one, the caretaker, the one who held everything together?

That's where the part was born. And it's been protecting you ever since. But here's the hard truth: the strategies that kept you safe then might be keeping you stuck now.

Why your nervous system fights rest

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how the nervous system gets stuck in survival responses. When you've experienced trauma, your body's threat detection system stays online. It's like a smoke alarm that never stops going off, even when there's no fire.

Rest requires your ventral vagal system to be active. That's the part of your nervous system that Stephen Porges identified as the "social engagement system." It's the state where you feel safe, connected, and able to be still. But if your nervous system has been conditioned to associate stillness with danger, rest becomes a threat.

Stay with me here. Your body doesn't know the difference between resting on your couch and resting in a war zone. If you grew up in an unpredictable environment, your body learned that vigilance equals survival. Letting your guard down could mean getting hurt. So your nervous system keeps you in a state of hyperarousal, even when you're physically safe.

That's why you can't just "decide" to rest. It's not a choice. It's a physiological response. And trying to override it with willpower usually makes things worse. I know because I tried. For years. I told myself I was just lazy. I pushed harder. I judged myself for not being able to relax. And all that did was create more shame, which made my nervous system even more activated.

Wild, right? The more I tried to force myself to rest, the less capable I was of actually resting.

How to work with the part, not against it

Here's what I've found actually helps. It's not about getting rid of the part that believes rest is dangerous. It's about building a relationship with it. Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems therapy, calls this "unblending." You separate from the part enough to see it clearly, to understand its role, to thank it for its service.

When I feel that drive to keep going, I stop. Not to force myself to rest, but to notice. I ask myself: "What's happening right now? What do I feel in my body?" Usually it's tightness in my chest. A knot in my stomach. A racing heart. I name it: "There's a part of me that's scared to stop."

Then I get curious. "What are you afraid would happen if I rested?" The answer is almost always something like: "You'd fall behind. You'd disappoint everyone. You'd feel the pain you've been avoiding. You'd disappear."

I don't argue with the part. I don't tell it it's wrong. I say: "I hear you. Thank you for protecting me. I know you're trying to keep me safe."

This might sound simple. It's not. It takes practice. The first time I tried it, I felt ridiculous. But over time, something shifted. The part started to relax. It didn't have to work so hard because someone was finally listening.

I also found it helpful to understand the mechanics of what was happening. A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Reading it helped me see that my drive wasn't a personal failing. It was a survival strategy I developed in response to a childhood where emotional safety was scarce.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It helped me understand that this part isn't the whole of me. It's just one part. And parts can change.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). This book taught me that I don't have to fix the part. I just have to accept that it's there, doing its best.

The slow work of learning to rest

I'm not going to tell you that I've fully healed this part. I haven't. Some days I still feel that drive to keep going, to prove my worth through productivity, to earn the right to exist by being useful. But I've learned to recognize it. And I've learned to negotiate.

Now, when I feel the urge to push past my limits, I don't ignore it. I don't fight it. I say: "I know you want me to keep working. I know you're scared. But I'm going to rest for 20 minutes. And then I'll check in with you."

Sometimes the part agrees. Sometimes it doesn't. But even when it doesn't, I've started to build trust. The part sees that I'm not abandoning it. I'm not pretending it doesn't exist. I'm just expanding the options.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman writes about the three stages of recovery: safety, remembrance and mourning, and reconnection. For those of us with parts that fear rest, safety has to come first. We have to convince our nervous systems that it's actually okay to stop.

That takes time. It takes repetition. It takes small doses of rest that don't trigger the alarm system. Maybe it's five minutes of sitting still. Maybe it's a walk without a destination. Maybe it's lying on the floor and feeling your breath move through your body.

Start small. The part will resist. That's okay. Notice the resistance. Thank it for showing up. And then try again tomorrow.

You're not broken for struggling to rest. You're not lazy. You're not defective. You're a person whose nervous system learned a hard lesson: that stillness is dangerous. And now you're learning a new one: that stillness can be safe.

It's slow work. It's uncomfortable work. But it's possible. And you don't have to do it alone.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance by Tara Brach (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay C. Gibson (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors by Janina Fisher (paid link)
